title: [Bug]: Oracle MySQL auto-create DDL fails for max-length table identifiers
summary: The Oracle MySQL DDL helper appended a primary-key suffix to table names, causing max-length table identifiers to exceed the identifier limit.
observed: A valid max-length table name could fail recommended DDL generation because the generated constraint name became too long.
expected: Auto-create DDL must generate valid deterministic constraint names for valid table names.
reproduction: Run the focused Oracle MySQL hardening regression test for max-length table DDL.
impact: Local test tables and disposable environments can fail even when the table identifier itself is valid.
delivery_semantics: The fix affects DDL generation only and does not change write or ACK behavior.
security: Deterministic bounded names keep auto-create behavior predictable without exposing environment details.
acceptance: Oracle MySQL DDL generation supports max-length valid table identifiers.
Generated primary-key names remain deterministic and within identifier limits.
tests: Run the focused Oracle MySQL hardening regression test and Oracle MySQL DDL tests.
documentation: No separate user documentation is required beyond existing auto-create guidance unless behavior is clarified in release notes.
closeout: Close only after the release containing the DDL fix is published.
